subject: [PATCH RFC v3 4/6] mm/gup: add get_user_page_vma() to fault in a page under a held lock

2026-07-17.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called get_user_page_vma() to the Linux kernel's mm/gup.c file. This function is used to fault in a single page under a held lock, either the mmap lock or the per-VMA lock. It simplifies the __get_user_pages() function and allows for more flexible locking behavior. The patch also includes changes to handle specific cases such as VM_IO/VM_PFNMAP VMA and userfaultfd VMA. The goal of this patch is to improve the handling of page faults in the kernel, allowing for more efficient and reliable memory access.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that faultin_page() may not hold the mmap lock when called with FOLL_VMA_LOCK, which could lead to a lock ordering violation, and requested clarification on this point.

Reviewer Usama Arif noted that check_vma_flags() returns early for VM_IO and VM_PFNMAP, allowing non-FOLL_FORCE callers to read COWed pages from PROT_NONE PFNMAP VMA, and requested restructing the function

Reviewer Usama Arif noted that __get_user_pages() calls flush_anon_page() and flush_dcache_page() before returning a page, and asked if the same should be done in get_user_page_vma().
reviews.raw_body:
Does the above line also need to be changed? mmap_lock is not held on entry in
faultin_page() if passing FOLL_VMA_LOCK, right?

---

Do you need to somehow restructure check_vma_flags()?

The first thing that check_vma_flags() does is:

	if (vm_flags & (VM_IO | VM_PFNMAP))
		return -EFAULT;

check_vma_flags() returns before evaluating VM_READ,
FOLL_FORCE, FOLL_ANON or the architecture permission check.

The code then ignores that early error and may return a COWed
page through follow_page_mask(). A non-FOLL_FORCE caller
can therefore read a COWed page from a PROT_NONE PFNMAP VMA.

---

__get_user_pages() does

	flush_anon_page(vma, subpage, start + j * PAGE_SIZE);
	flush_dcache_page(subpage);

before returning the page. Do you need to that here as well above?

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the current behavior of falling back to ->access() hook is correct, even though the implementation needs improvement. They proposed changing check_vma_flags() to ignore specified flags, specifically VM_IO | VM_PFNMAP, to achieve this.

Author acknowledged a potential issue on systems with virtually indexed caches and expressed interest in restructuring get_user_page_vma_remote() to avoid double VMA lookup.
reviews.raw_body:
In this code path, if we return an error to
__access_remote_vm(), we end up falling back
to the ->access() hook, and will end up reading
the memory that's "under" that COW, instead of
the COWed page that is currently in the process
we are accessing.

In other words, I think the behavior of this
code is the way we want, even though the
implementation should be cleaned up.

How about we change check_vma_flags() so it
ignores specified flags?

check_vma_flags(vma, gup_flags, ignore_flags)

Then this one call to check_vma_flags can have
it ignore VM_IO | VM_PFNMAP, since we want to
access the COWed pages in those VMAs in this
code path, but fall back to ->access when
there are no COW pages.

---

I suppose on systems with virtually indexed caches
we do need that.

Thanks for spotting that!

Now I also wonder if I could restructure get_user_page_vma_remote()
to do the lookup before the get_user_pages_remote(), and have
it then use the new get_user_page_vma() function, to avoid
the double VMA lookup (why are we doing that, anyway?)

2026-07-20.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called get_user_page_vma() to the Linux kernel's memory management system. It is designed to fault in a single page under a held lock, either the mmap lock or the per-VMA lock. The function is intended for use by __access_remote_vm(), which needs to access a page that has already been looked up and locked. The patch includes several changes to existing functions, including get_gate_page() and faultin_page(), to support this new functionality. It also introduces a new flag called FOLL_VMA_LOCK to indicate when the per-VMA lock is being used. The patch aims to simplify the process of accessing pages under a held lock, making it easier for kernel developers to write efficient and safe code.
